replies.author: @GhostsStoryGaming
replies.text:
​@gamerred691 To the extent that 
1. Shouldnt have to watch another piece of media, you can either recap, or bring the audience up to speed either as an actual intro, or by way of a scene conveying it, preferably without it being straight exposition.
2. Pretty much what this character has been built for, much like Shuri, to be better. Smarter. More creative.
The lack? Honestly? Probably just the ability to empathize with the character itself. From Riri to Ironheart, the character has to be likeable. "Ah she's a stupid kid, she's gotta figure it out" is fine until you're covering your face to hide from the terrible capability of a writer to do anything remotely creative with that character other than blame inexperience and naivete for why they aren't able to logically maneuver through the obstacles that plague them, as a genius. As a fictional heroine we're supposed to want to back. Imperfections are great, character arcs are wonderful, but you have to get people invested first, then confront the issues. Cut out the first 15 minutes of UP and him refusing to sell his house as the first point you see him just leaves a character who is obstinate for no reason. A crotchety old fella who is just waiting to die but wants to stick it to the man one last time.

As many content review channels, this one included, have pointed out is that there are numerous avenues to which an issue may be solved. The issue is and will always be, the story is only as smart as a writer. It's why playing an 18INT character in D&D is hilarious because most people couldn't fathom the thought process of someone at 14INT. 

Why is THIS problem the one that must be struggled through without alternative? Why is this problem the one that shapes a person? How is this problem going to continue to impact this person beyond the now? What will this problem provide once overcome? If you cannot create a labyrinth for your character to struggle in, then all you're left with is a spiral with only one obvious choice of direction, that isn't clever, it isn't hard, it just takes time to get through.

author: @xhalox471
text:
Marvel is completely off the rails. Characters like the Blood Brothers—who were originally male alien twins—are now reimagined as twin lesbians. Slug, a crime boss known for being a grotesquely obese white man in the comics, is now suddenly portrayed as a flamboyant, black female character. That’s not creative adaptation—it’s blatant agenda-pushing.

I’m not here to support the constant focus on identity politics—gay, feminist, or otherwise. People can live how they want, but when every show shoves it front and center, it ruins the story. Ironheart feels like a product built around a checklist instead of heart. The writing is weak, the visuals are rushed, and it disrespects the source material.

And when Marvel does legacy handoffs now, they feel forced. Unlike Iron Man to Spider-Man—which felt earned—this just feels like replacing characters with boxes to tick. At this point, I watch Marvel content out of curiosity to see just how bad it’s going to get.🤦🏾‍♂️🤦🏾‍♂️

The difference is that MCU Iron Man when faced with many of those same issues was not excused for any of them, and went through a character arc to undo them. Stark's immediate reaction to finding out that his company sold weapons to terror groups was "This is my fault, i have to fix it, and i will fix it", rather than try to make excuses to justify any of it. I don't recall a single character throughout the entirety of the MCU ever praising him for doing anything wrong or bad, instead consistently calling him out harshly for whenever he did. replies.text:
@throatwobblermangrove8510 It’s wild how a conversation about a Black character, created as Black in the comics, Riri Williams, somehow spiraled into a meltdown about Anne Boleyn, Cleopatra, and fictional African nations. That’s not debate, that’s deflection.

You claim you’d love to see “us” in entertainment, but when we show up whether in sci fi, fantasy, or historical fiction, you suddenly need it to be “legitimate” by your definition. The moment we’re centered, you cry appropriation. But no one’s stealing anything when the character was ours to begin with. Ironheart is a Black girl genius in the comics. So what exactly are you mad about?

Your rant about “participation trophies” is just a dressed up way of saying you think Black visibility is unearned unless it comes with your stamp of approval. And don’t pretend to be interested in Mansa Musa when the only time you bring up Black history is to scold us for what you think we should be celebrating. Truth is, you’re less interested in seeing us represented and more interested in controlling how we’re allowed to exist on screen.

Let’s be honest: if the issue was really about “accuracy,” you wouldn’t be foaming at the mouth over a fantasy film or a comic book character. This isn’t about history. It’s about your discomfort seeing Blackness unfiltered, unashamed, and unapologetically taking up space in stories, in culture, in media.

And if that bothers you more than actual injustice? That says a lot more about you than it does about us.
